blurb: An airy cloud of wiry wands that dance in the slightest breeze, each tipped with four-petaled flowers that open white and age to pink — like a hovering swarm of tiny butterflies from early summer until frost.
water: Low; xeric and heat-proof once its taproot is established
spread: Clumping from a deep taproot; the species self-sows (many named cultivars are sterile)
seasons: Slender flower wands of white-to-pink blooms from early summer to hard frost; a low basal rosette of narrow, sometimes maroon-spotted leaves; little winter presence (cut back)
wildlife: Long-blooming nectar for bees, butterflies and hummingbirds; larval host for the white-lined sphinx moth
toxic: No significant toxicity reported to people or pets
origin: South-central US — Louisiana, Texas & northern Mexico
habitat: Open prairies, pinelands and pond edges on lean, well-drained ground

care.hardiness: Root-hardy through Boulder's zone 5b–6a — rated roughly USDA 5a–9b — so it reliably overwinters here, though it is a short-lived perennial best replaced or left to self-sow every few years.[1]
care.planting: Set plants out in spring after the mid-May last frost so the taproot establishes over the warm season. It resents wet feet, so plant on a well-drained or slightly raised site; in containers give it a gritty free-draining mix, water sparingly, and expect it to be short-lived in a pot since the taproot wants room to run deep.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun for the densest flowering and sturdiest stems; it tolerates a little afternoon shade but grows floppier and blooms less.[1][2]
care.soil: Adapts to sandy, loamy, or clay soils as long as drainage is good — lean ground is ideal. Rich, heavily amended, or poorly drained soil causes lax leafy growth, fewer flowers, and crown/root rot.[1]
care.water: Highly drought and heat tolerant once established — the deep taproot carries it through Front Range summers on little to no supplemental water. Overwatering, especially in winter, is the main way to kill it.[1]
care.spacing: Space 18–24 in apart; the wiry stems weave together into an airy, see-through veil that never needs staking when grown hard in lean soil and full sun.[1]
care.propagation: Grow the species from seed (it self-sows readily) or take basal cuttings; established clumps can be divided, though the deep taproot makes division tricky and slow to recover. Named cultivars are often sterile and are propagated by cuttings to stay true.[1]
care.bloom: Flowers open a few at a time up each wand from early summer until the first hard frost, opening white and blushing pink as they age (pink-flowered cultivars exist). The true species flowers heavily without deadheading; deadheading or a midsummer shear tidies floppy stems and can spur a fresh flush.[1][2]
care.feeding: Needs no feeding — it flowers best in lean soil. Fertilizer or rich compost just pushes soft foliage at the expense of bloom and makes the stems flop.[1]
care.maintenance: Very low care: cut the spent flower wands and old foliage back to the basal rosette in late fall or early spring. Where the species self-sows more than you want, shear off the wands before seed sets or pull the easy-to-spot seedlings.[1]
care.selfsow: The straight species reseeds freely and can naturalize into a drift — welcome in a wild planting, easy to curb by deadheading before seed set; most named garden cultivars are sterile and stay put.[1]
care.troubles: Almost trouble-free in lean, sunny, well-drained ground. The usual problems trace back to too much water or richness — root and crown rot in soggy soil, and floppy, sparse-blooming plants in shade or fertile beds.[1]

references.name: NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Oenothera lindheimeri
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Oenothera lindheimeri

edible.summary: Grown as an ornamental — no part of gaura is used as food, though it is not considered dangerously poisonous.[1]
edible.caution: There is no documented culinary use of gaura and no authority lists it as an edible plant, so treat it as inedible and enjoy it only as a garden flower. It is not reported to be significantly toxic to people or pets, but 'not a food' is the safe assumption without a trusted edible-use source.[1][2]
